summary: Gruber covers Pebble founder Eric Migicovsky's effort to revive the Pebble smartwatch after convincing Google to open-source the Pebble OS firmware. Migicovsky, who sold his messaging startup Beeper to Automattic, is starting a new company to build a smartwatch with Pebble's core values: e-paper display, long battery life, physical buttons, and hackability. Gruber draws an analogy to Panic's Playdate handheld as a model for achievable niche success. He uses the occasion to argue passionately that the tech world needs more small-scale, soul-driven projects rather than trillion-dollar moonshots, comparing indie hardware to indie filmmaking and art.
key_points: Google agreed to open-source the Pebble OS firmware, making the revival possible despite the code sitting dormant for years
Migicovsky's wishlist — e-paper, long battery, buttons, hackability — represents a deliberate rejection of mainstream smartwatch design priorities
Gruber compares the potential to Playdate: a beloved niche product rather than a mass-market competitor
The tech industry needs more projects that aim to be great million-dollar ideas rather than mediocre billion-dollar ones
Computer hardware and software can and should aspire to be art, not just products
money_quotes: The whole tech world needs more projects that aren't trying to become billion- (let alone trillion‑) dollar ideas, but are happily shooting for success as million-dollar ideas (or less!).
If your list of all-time favorite movies doesn't include a bunch that were made on shoestring budgets, your whole list probably sucks, because you have no taste.
Indie art is often great art, imbued by the souls and obsessions of its creators, and blockbuster art is often garbage art, imbued only by soulless corporate bureaucracies.
Computer hardware and software can be — ought to be — attempts at creating art.
My second idea: Quixote — because this isn't going to be a hit this time either.
If their goal is to be to smartwatches what Playdate is to handheld gaming, that's definitely achievable, and if they succeed, will by definition be a lot of fun.
key_insight: The tech industry's obsession with trillion-dollar scale has crowded out the kind of small, passion-driven hardware projects that produce genuinely beloved products — and a Pebble revival could be a worthy example of indie tech as art.
